comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) I have a question about the breathing relaxation exercise in LRM. Given the link with breathing to the etheric plane, does the exercise work on the etheric plane? I feel more relaxed, but physically I feel about the same, if that makes any sense.

2) I’ve recently started realizing that a lot of my problems are related to being a muddle of contradictory desires, ideals which can’t both be expressed, and contradictory beliefs, to say nothing of the mess when more than one of those interacts! How do you address this sort of muddledness? The two techniques I know of are journaling and occult practices, but are there others I can use to reduce the extent to which I am contradictory?

3) Is there an upper limit to how much journaling can be done at a time? I think it’ll be the lesser of the physical, practical, or emotional limits: as long as your hand can handle it, you have the time for it, and you can handle it emotionally, it’s fine. Are there other issues to watch for?

4) I grew up rich (my parents insist they are “middle class”, but their tax bill, after making all the deductions possible, is higher than the average income in the city we live in [which is one of the richest in the country]), but I now have a reasonably low income and a lot of debt, and my family has made it basically impossible for me to live with them. I’ve tried to address this with a budget, but can’t seem to keep to one, so I’ve started journaling on it and found it has been because my subconscious has been using the following logic:

Poor people budget.
Thus, if I budget I’m poor; if I don’t, then I’m not poor.
I have lots of debt, so I can’t afford to be poor.
Therefore I can’t afford to make and stick to a budget.

Is this level of absurdity normal for the origins of a self-defeating pattern?

5) I’m learning to notice the subtle planes, and have discovered that the area around my apartment is ethericly filthy, while astrally tolerable, while the suburb I grew up in is astrally filthy, but ethericly tolerable. On the physical plane, meanwhile, both are about as clean as any urban neighbourhood ever gets.

This got me wondering what’s different about the two neighbourhoods, and this is what I’ve come up with: the suburb has plenty of vegetation: lots of trees, tons of grass on people’s lawns, plenty of parks, etc. The neighbourhood around my apartment is in an urban core, and full of concrete, apartment buildings, and lacking in vegetation. Thus, I think the presence of loads of plants helps explain the relative cleanliness on the etheric plane for the suburb.

The suburb meanwhile is full of people having mental breakdowns over the end of progress and over the rise of right wing populism, which probably pollutes the astral; meanwhile it’s home to a wild amount of conspicuous over-consumption, and it doesn’t seem like indulging in that would be very good for the local astral environment either. Most people around my apartment are poor, and just trying to make ends meet: the lack of conspicuous over indulgence and middle class entitlement that dominates the suburb seems to mean that astrally things are cleaner here.

a) Does this sound reasonable to you?

b) Does cleanliness on one plane help at all with cleanliness on another? It seems weird that conditions can be so different from plane to plane, but then again they are discrete and not continuous....

replies.content:
ecosophia JMG, Would you mind double checking what follows and making a comment on the validity/safety of it? I'd hate to have someone hurt out of my own over-eagerness to help.

Hey, Anonymous. Maybe it would be useful for you to think this again from an oblique angle?

>Poor people budget.
>Thus, if I budget I’m poor; if I don’t, then I’m not poor.
>I have lots of debt, so I can’t afford to be poor.
>Therefore I can’t afford to make and stick to a budget

Budgets require skill, and it is a learning slope... so why don't you address the debt side of the equation first?

I have lots of debt, so I can't afford to be poor.
Poor people budget, so I cannot afford to budget.
Because I will to budget, today I wage war on debt.
I can, want and do kill debt, flatten debt, vanquish debt.

p.d. Just a word of caution, about the "no quarter" part. I suspect it is likely that if you use this as an Affirmation (or perhaps even if you accept it silently), Fate is going to take you on it and throw you some opportunities to "not ask quarter"... and that will be not pleasant to you.
I'd recommend for you to meditate on the fact that you'd be "waging war on debt" not "getting into a bar brawl with debt". In alignment with the above, you should actively pursue opportunities to take on small amounts of "strategic debt" that allow you to squash even more debt, either immediately or in the near future (i.e. buying a cheap stove and kitchenware on credit, so that you can cut your food bill). That way, you will not pin yourself in a corner and end up getting hit by an emergency where a little bit of debt would have avoided a lot of misery.

comments.title: Competition Morality
comments.content:
I believe in competition. The ability to impartially discern the effectiveness of behavior. I am an athlete, once at the collegiate national level. Besides practice, sleep, food, breath, and emotions, I used a variety of visualizations to improve my performance. Usually my visualizations focused on improving efficiency, and this improved my performance significantly. A few times I used visualizations to siphon energy off of my competition, and this did improve my performance slightly, though I never calculated whether there was a measurable diminishment of others capability.

My question, for the commentariat as well: Is it karmically detrimental to use body knowledge to harm your competition in a test of physical ability.

Also, I do not believe the raspberry jam principle applies here because this is energy work, not magic.
Also, in competition there is always an arms-race, and in this case if the result was greater awareness and protection against what is commonly considered non-physical, then I would be very happy. It would make the world healthier for all of us.

That said, using visualizations of various kinds to enhance performance is, I believe, well known in most branches of athletics, so what you were trying to do to your opponents, they were quite possibly doing to you as well. As long as it's not forbidden by the rules of competition, and all sides can use it freely, it's not a significant karmic issue.

replies.title: Re: Competition Morality
replies.content:
"My question, for the commentariat as well: Is it karmically detrimental to use body knowledge to harm your competition in a test of physical ability."

There is, ultimately, only one will in the living universe and by interfering with the will of others, you diminish your access to the will and your own willpower.

Instead, I suggest you visualize everyone doing their best. This may not help win an individual match in the short term, but in the long run it will help you to learn from your competition, and be better liked by rivals as well as team members--and to like yourself better too.

Maintaining a willingness to help everyone do their best (including yourself), will give you access to higher order of willpower, which enhances all visualization and magickal workings.

I have found competition to be an access point to greater cooperation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"horror and murder mystery writers do a lot of thinking in unpleasant directions, so what advice would you give them?"

I am a horror writer and lover of magick.

A good horror story is a moral parable where the protagonists never learns their lesson and has to suffer as a result. They either face a horrific fate at the end or a temporary victory, tainted by the assurance that the evil will return. Remember that karma, from a magickal perspective, is as certain as gravity and not a matter of relative values.

The caution is to pass on moral lessons you yourself have learned, because otherwise, you are inviting such lessons into your life. I have found this to be a powerful motivator for spiritual development--when spirit calls, I drop everything and listen, because I am horrified at the idea of acting otherwise.

Keep in mind that the story events are set, but the story premise is conditional. A thriller involves a character being set upon unjustly (and usually triumphing heroically), a horror involves a character being set upon justly. If you feel you'd fare better than the protagonist in the same situation, contemplating the consequences of acting otherwise (the horror story) can strengthen your resolve.

The real horror is the empathy we experience for characters who haven't learned their karmic lessons. This is a bittersweet experience, and I write for it, because I long for the wisdom hidden therein.

comments.title: Cumulative skills
comments.content:
Hey hey JMG,

Someone commented recently that musical skill is cumulative through incarnations. It seems like other skills should behave in similar ways, but obviously some don't, for example remembering a previous life doesn't give one access to speaking another language.

Thanks,

replies.title: Re: Cumulative skills

replies.title: Re: Cumulative skills
replies.content:
If there are such things as past lives,* then some of mine were devoted to rigorous historical-philological scholarship: its methods came naturally to me at a very early age--well before I was 10--as did the urge to research the obscurest possible matters, and I had already developed a fair amount of skill in them by the time I left high school for college. Another carry-over would be a facility for learning dead languages. (Living languages bore me, though they are useful as scholarly tools.)
_________
* Schopenhauer had another, and to my mind quite satisfactory, explanation for the same real phenomena that lead to theories of reincarnation and past lives.

replies.title: Re: Cumulative skills
replies.content: From a reincarnationist perspective, that does sound like the childhood of someone who'd devoted several previous lives to scholarship! I'll have reread Schopenhauer sometime soon -- did he deal with the phenomenon of remarkable talent, as well as apparent memories? To my mind, Mozart and people like him are among the best arguments for reincarnation -- he only makes sense if you grant the possibility that he'd spent several previous lives in music, and brought the capacities from those lives forward into his life as Mozart.

replies.title: Re: Cumulative skills
replies.content:
I found Schopenhauer's alternate explanation in his very profound "Essay on Spirit-Seering and Everything Connected Therewith" (Versuch über das Geistersehen und was damit zusammenhängt), published in 1851.

He says--roughly--that our world of time and space, matter and energy, is a human construct, and that everything that we perceive as happening in it, whether in the past, the present, or the future, is co-present in an existence that knows neither time nor space. We cannot access that existence through our bodily senses, but we have other, non-bodily senses as well, which we rarely use, and use less and less as we age. It is through these non-bodily senses that we may perceive, for example, people and their actions elsewhere (or elsewhen). These perceptions usually get assigned to our presernt time and place, so that (for example) a man in Russia in 1812 may seem to us to be present in our own home in 2020, doing whatever he was doing in 1812. A recently born small child will still be using these non-bodily senses quite freely, and may happen to perceive and remember an entire life from some other place and time. And what he perceives and takes into himself is not just a view of the external actions of that person, but the whole internal life of that person as it is unfolding. What the child absorbs and remembers of this, he supposes happened to him, since he knows so little of other people at so young an age. If he can recall and relate what he has remembered, and if it can be chacked against old records fo accuracy, the child's unexpected--seemingly impossible--knowledge of that other time and place will appear to show that he is something like a later incarnation of that long-ago person. (I have gone somewhat beyond the consequences here that Schopenhauer draws from his premises, but it follows from his premises as readily as the conclusions that he does draw.)

I'm not saying that reincarnation doesn't happen, only that the phemonena that are adduced as proof of reincarnation can be generally accounted for satisfactorily without assumning reincarnation to have happened. (My family though that reincarnation could occur, but that there was no single, unitary process that almost all people would go through after their deaths.)

replies.title: Re: Cumulative skills
replies.content: I read it in English myself, in the 1974 English translation of his collected essays, "Parerga and Paralipomena."  It's not on line, but I'm sending you a pdf that I made of the published translation of the essay.

replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: Chaos in China
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thanks so much for hosting these discussions. Here are some things I've been thinking about.

Item 1: In China and other Asian cultures, there's a belief in so-called "Dragon Veins." These are akin to ley lines, geographic energy meridians that connect sacred sites and nourish the land. China's biggest vein by some accounts is the Yangtze River.

Item 2: In 2006 China finished building the Three Gorges Dam on the Yangtze River. This dam is the largest hydroelectric project in history. Its construction was controversial even among the Chinese congress, who passed it in a 2/3 vote (infrastructure projects normally pass with over 90%). Many engineers and experts testified against the dam's construction on the basis that it's built on a soft limestone formation, it sits on the lakebed rather than being anchored in bedrock, it will see excessive silt buildup, it will actually make flooding worse, and other reasons. From a metaphysical standpoint, the dam neatly severs China's main vital current.

Item 3: This year the Three Gorges Dam has been battered by some of the heaviest flooding in modern Chinese history. In order to prevent the dam from failing, the Chinese government has flooded and destroyed dozens of towns along the Yangtze, both upstream and downstream from the dam, in order to divert water that would otherwise put pressure on the dam. Heavy losses of life and property have ensued, but it's doubtful the government will reveal specifics. If the dam fails, 10 billion cubic meters of water will be released and destroy many cities along the Yangtze, including Wuhan and possibly even Shanghai, as well as most of China's farmland and 15% of the world's manufacturing capacity. Over 300 million people occupy the area that would be affected. The deliberate flooding has already ruined many farms.

Item 4: At the beginning of the year, before COVID-19 or any of the other oncoming disasters became apparent, the Chinese government announced its intent to rewrite the Bible and Koran to support communism. Such an act happens to be one of the ultimate sins according to those books.

Item 5: Even aside from the Yangtze flooding and COVID-19, China is enduring a Biblical panoply of disasters this year. This includes flooding and destruction on the east coast due to typhoons Dindo and Hagupit, egg-sized hail in Inner Mongolia, snow in August in Beijing, a plague of locusts in southwest China, a plague of ticks in Wuhan, explosions and fires at many factories (sabotage?), natural gas explosions, a new tick-borne virus and a new swine flu apparently more deadly than COVID-19, prompting the slaughter of most of China's hogs. The Yellow River is flooding as well, and droughts are occurring in other parts of the country. Furthermore, during a recent meeting of the Chinese Communist Party in Beijing, stormclouds came over the city so thickly that they turned the sky dark as night at 3pm. There are also violent protests against some local governments, and a significant fraction of Chinese gold reserves used as collateral for loans have been found to be counterfeit.

Item 6: The chaos mages at 4chan have taken a strong interest in the Three Gorges Dam, dedicating a long series of discussion threads to monitoring the situation. These threads are also dedicated to gathering energy and focusing intent. The goal: you guessed it, to make the dam collapse. Posters "roll for digits" to bring the dam down, interpreting consecutive digits in post numbers as signs of favor from Kek, the chaotic Egyptian frog god that they have taken as patron. They also post erotic images of cartoon girls who represent the dam and the flood. A common theme is the flood girl raping the dam girl, with her orgasm representing the collapse of the dam due to the oncoming flood. These images are used as foci in sex magic, with users dedicating their own orgasms to breaking the dam. Users also post mocking graphics, prose and songs to direct energy through humor in gleeful anticipation of China's ruin.

Item 7: 4chan posters aren't the only ones focusing intent against China. Following the Chinese crackdown on Hong Kong, protesters there have been chanting the slogan "Heaven Will Destroy the Chinese Communists," invoking the classical Chinese concept of the Mandate of Heaven that rulers of the land must uphold.

Questions: Are China's woes the result of a magical attack? Divine retribution? China's rise to power has been meteoric, and now their fortunes have taken a 180. The black sky for the Party meeting seems like the worst of omens. Is it wise to perform magical workings to preserve the Three Gorges Dam, given that the dam is effectively a wound in China's spiritual fabric? Does the best karmic path avoid the situation entirely?

In my own experience, Dot, there isn't a form of divination on the planet that can't sometimes be taken over by a malevolent or trickster God or Spirit. That sort of Being actively seeks out vulnerable humans to mess with, just for the sheer joy of causing them misery. And there's nothing more vulnerable anywhere than a mother who has started to think that her child might die soon.

The fact that you didn't "know what got into" you very strongly suggests that you're been set up and are being messed with by such a Being. That Being, whatever It is, first led your child to say things (in all a child's innocence) which were specifically designed as subtle triggers for your natural fears as a mother. Then It worked on your fears directly, so as to make you throw all prudence to the winds and do that divination. That was the hard part for It. The easy part was for It to rig your divinations so you would get the answer you feared most.

Your best response, I think, is to rage with all your might at the Being who played this very nasty trick on you. Name It with some foul name of your own devising, curse It out at length with every ounce of inventiveness and strength you've got, yell at it to get the [undruidly word beginning with "f"] out of your life, and inform It that whether your child lives or not is up to you, not to It, and you'll be [another undruidly word beginning with "d"] if you let It have any say in the matter. Keep on doing this sort of thing until you've really run out of steam. (If you can really get sufficient privacy, you can even do all this out loud. But it works just as well if you do it inside your head alone, not out loud. It will be just as effective either way.)

If I'm right that such a Being has been messing with you, that's really all it takes to send It packing. They are cowards, and very much afraid of focused human wrath. Once you've sent it packing, you should be able to put those divinations out of your mind entirely, just as if you had never done them at all.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Dot,

I like Robert's reply to this, but I'll add my own $.02

Some years ago, I had a hypnagogia episode-- a clearly audible voice-- which suggested to me that my then-boyfriend would not reach old age. Memorable.

I didn't know what to make of it. It wasn't sinister, I hadn't asked. Had the flavor of a sympathetic fair warning. I'm not inclined to simply write these things off. I have never, before or since, heard voices.

To this day, I have no idea whether it spoke truth or not. I thought it through, and it didn't matter. Married him anyway. In the end, these things are not in our control. Bad things happen, and nobody gets a special exemption card. I lost two close family members in a freak accident, and struggled mightily with this. It was the most terrible thing. And yet... if you read the news, you see that it happens to someone, somewhere, every day. It's comforting I guess to imagine that it only happens to other people, but that's a lie. Why me? Why not me? It is the human experience.

Perhaps it told the truth. Perhaps it lied. But it's not like marrying someone else would guarantee a long, safe, tragedy-free life. It is what it is. Because I don't know... maybe I appreciate my husband a little bit more, because there is always that little reminder. I accept the possibility, and try to make sure I don't squander the time, in case it's short. But I don't dwell on it.

I hope this isn't too morbid. Pray for your loved ones. For the most part, you don't get a choice about how long they live, and anyone could be taken from you at any time. But that's just life. We live anyway.

comments.title: Source of morals
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have been struggling with the question of where morals originate. For many people, they point to a scriptural source, which seems problematic. Another source might be from spiritual experience, but that could be highly individualistic and contradictory.

I have an intuition that morals come from higher planes, and have to do with the evolution of the individual, but am having trouble getting a grip on it. Could you point me in the right direction here?

replies.title: Re: Source of morals
replies.content:
Well, I wasn't around when they were invented, you know... ;-)

My take has always been that they're simply the rules that human societies come up with to regulate human affairs. The word "morals" comes from a Latin word that means "customs, habits." (Interestingly, the word "ethics" comes from a Greek word that means exactly the same thing.) That's why moral intuitions vary so drastically from culture to culture -- why for example, extramarital sex is a huge issue in some cultures, a minor issue in others, and no issue at all in still others. Being human traditions that have evolved over time, they contain a good deal of wisdom mixed up with a good deal of folly, and the evolution of the individual always involves both understanding when it's wise to accept your culture's moral ideas and when it's necessary to reject them.

Regarding the differences between degradation, sublimation, and allowing something to spin off into Ring-Chaos, I'm just having difficulty with understanding when and how I'd apply each of these tools practically in regular life and practice. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. You degrade something when you want to lock it into place. If you want to establish the habit of meditating daily, for example, take two emotional states -- for example, vanity (wanting to imagine yourself as becoming an enlightened being) and frustration (with your self-defeating habits) and use them alternately to goad yourself into meditating. They'll become locked together, and the force in them will degrade to the plane of habit.

You sublimate something when you want to dissolve something in yourself. If you want to get rid of the habit of wasting time on the internet, for example, pay attention to the emotions that keep the habit in place. Once you've identified them, find ways to express them individually -- not together -- and the habit will gradually dissolve.

You step aside and let something go to the Ring-Chaos when it's not part of you and you want to get rid of it. If you want to keep online ads from influencing your thinking, for example, get a good ad blocker and then don't patronize any website that demands that you whitelist them.

Does that help any?

replies.title: Re: Astral Rejection
replies.content:
The countermeasures I'm familiar with all use misdirection. For example, you get a bunch of people to visualize something that doesn't actually exist, and since your remote viewer is seeing things on the astral plane -- thus the uneven results of remote viewing -- he's going to see the astral form you built up and report back to Moscow or whoever on that, rather than what's actually there. Still, it's not impossible that other countermeasures have been devised.

The astral body is hard to damage. The etheric body is much more vulnerable -- pierce it with a highly conductive metal such as silver, and whoever was etherically projecting will be found stone cold dead.

replies.title: Re: The Guardian at the Threshold

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Related to the question about the morality of magic in competitions, there are some things I wonder about. (I don't know which of these would make suitable questions, but I numbered them in case some of them do.)

1) Sometimes it seems to me like it would make sense to pray or do workings for cultural leaders to be free from harmful influences, even (or especially) if they are cultural leaders who use their platforms to advocate against people believing in those influences, which gets in the way both of learning to protect against them and of learning to avoid creating them in the first place. But then I wonder if this would be partly like "cooking the books" in favor of those leaders' associated cultures, the equivalent of propping up a dysfunctional regional industry with subsidies. But at the same time, because of division of labor, maybe it's better for everyone if there are people who are highly motivated to develop the skills for dealing with material things, even when that means those people have to disbelieve in everything else, and that they could therefore only be protected by other people who did believe. Maybe a good way to balance these considerations would be if the intent of the prayer/working were adjusted, so it was about freeing the target from everything except for things that right now it would be good for them to develop responsibility for.

2) The same might go for oneself. I have a hard time feeling like the kind of daily protective practice suggested here is a good idea in the long run if I don't adjust it in that same direction.

3) I'd like to know if there are good myths about someone who shows wisdom and cunning by changing the terms of a competition to make special accommodation for otherwise disadvantaged contestants, not so that their preferred contestants win or lose, but so that the outcome is a useful surprise even to them.

4a) Sometimes I worry if the laws and conditions of the physical world are the result of a long and expensive wrangling and mutual-binding over the rules for making a fair test (or unbiased examples) of something, the same way the rules of market economies or freedom of speech are. I don't know how I would tell whether learning magic/occultism/religious practice had always been something intrinsically questionable, like learning how to bribe contest officials, evade environmental regulations, perform putsches, or get the state to promote one's own religion and persecute others. This picture might, after all, explain why magic seems to have such limited direct influence. And trying to get answers about whether this were true or false by occult means might not get you the whole story, any more than you could get an unbiased picture of the motives for political secularism from the 19th-century Catholic Church.

But there are also fundamental considerations pointing the other way. If it's something that everyone is unconsciously doing at a low level anyway, it seems like it would be unsustainable to try to build a world on the premise that it never happens. Paraphrasing the usual 2nd Amendment slogan: "If gaining advantage by understanding the hidden structure behind the outward forms of power in the world is branded immoral, only those branded immoral will gain advantage..." And it would be dangerous to depend on the health of a tree while refusing to understand the roots beneath. Maybe the wrangling might have turned out wrong somehow, or never really finished, or it built everything on the assumption that there would always be live contention, so maybe the only way to keep things in good shape would be if there were an ongoing process of people who were in contact with the same original motives of both selfish advantage and fairness, and the same kind of knowledge of how to act on those motives by working toward establishing different structures. I don't know how I would find out about whether this were true in "higher" worlds, but that's the picture I get when I try to understand the context behind outward forms of political and cultural power in "this" world.

Maybe the best anyone could do to figure out the truth about this is a "by their fruits" kind of thing.

4b) On the other hand, maybe if someone tried, just within the outwardly manifest world, to keep the relation between the outward forms of power and the hidden structure behind them on course, then this could be a sort of magical/collective-theurgical practice that would also have beneficial effects on the relation between the outwardly manifest world and the hidden structure behind that. And in this case, it might be better for that person to mostly avoid using occult influences, to avoid creating a biased feedback, like if a committee ruling on the medical efficacy of a psychoactive drug were doing their deliberations while under the influence of that drug. Some Buddhists say that the siddhis are mostly counterproductive distractions from the central project of cutting off clinging ghosts and delusions, and Dion Fortune's cosmology describes a risk of getting one's connection to nearer upper planes involved in a circular pattern of bondage where lower planes are substituted for farther upper planes as a source of guidance.

5) You've mentioned concluding that disbelief in the occult is the result of a malignant enchantment. Maybe all of the above is me being too charitable about the motives of whatever would have erected such an enchantment, but also, maybe that kind of charitable analysis is what it would take to pick out the thread of necessity or spark of positive intention that it might have been built around. Like Chesterton's fence, it could be something that will resist counterworkings more strongly if the worker doesn't demonstrate that they understand the good it was meant to accomplish.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It seems to me that you're massively overthinking this. You could as well argue that it's unfair to vote for the best candidate for mayor since other towns have bad mayors, or that it's unfair for you to come in out of the rain to avoid catching a cold because not everyone has that much sense.

As for "myths about someone who shows wisdom and cunning by changing the terms of a competition to make special accommodation for otherwise disadvantaged contestants, not so that their preferred contestants win or lose, but so that the outcome is a useful surprise even to them," as far as I know, there are none, and for good reason. When you do this, what happens is not that the better competitors get "a useful surprise," but that they recognize that the competition was not fair, that they were cheated, and that you can't be trusted.

And as for the rest -- hey, if you don't want to practice magic or take up religion, then by all means do something else. You don't have to be so baroque about it!

replies.content:
Well, I had meant it more like, the contest organizers themselves are among those who get the useful surprise, and they announce the changes far enough in advance.

Anyway, thank you for the time engaging with my concerns.
